Developer Productivity Brief: This gives you enough context to understand the assignment even if you have never worked on a software engineering product before.

__EMPTY: A developer is someone who builds and changes software. They write code, review code, test changes, and ship releases with a team.
__EMPTY_1: The product you build should help this user make better decisions.

__EMPTY: SDLC stands for Software Development Life Cycle. In simple terms: plan work, pick up a ticket, write code, review code, test it, deploy it, and fix issues after release.
__EMPTY_1: The metrics in this assignment come from these SDLC steps.

Developer Productivity Brief: Why do teams use developer productivity metrics?
__EMPTY: Good teams use metrics to find bottlenecks, improve quality, and make delivery healthier. They should not use them blindly to judge people.
__EMPTY_1: Your solution should feel helpful, not punitive.

Developer Productivity Brief: What is the actual product challenge?
__EMPTY: Metrics tell you what happened. A better product helps the user interpret those metrics and decide what to do next.
__EMPTY_1: This is the key problem you are solving.

__EMPTY: What it means in this workbook
Average time from PR opened to successful production deployment.
__EMPTY_1: Tells how long a change takes to reach users once the review process begins.

__EMPTY: Average time from issue moved to In Progress to issue marked Done.
__EMPTY_1: Shows how long work stays active once development starts.

__EMPTY: Escaped production bugs found in the month divided by issues completed in the month.
__EMPTY_1: Shows whether speed is hurting quality.

__EMPTY: Count of successful production deployments in the month.
__EMPTY_1: Frequent safe releases often indicate a healthier delivery loop.

__EMPTY: Count of merged pull requests in the month.
__EMPTY_1: Useful as an activity signal, but weak if used without quality and speed metrics.
